patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking project coordinator. Start with: “My pee smells really sweet and kind of fruity, and it’s been going on for a while.” Early on, volunteer: sometimes foamy urine, increased thirst and urination, and worry it’s diabetes. If asked, explain it’s been on/off since childhood, worse after sugary foods/soda, no burning/fever/back pain, prior urine dipsticks showed “sugar” but blood sugars were normal, and you feel more tired but functioning. Do not mention unless specifically asked about dental/oral, underwear, weight, or sexual concerns: more cavities, dry mouth at night, sticky underwear after peeing, mild unintentional weight loss from cutting carbs. Only if asked directly about intimacy or unusual behaviors, admit embarrassment: partner noticed sweet smell after sex, you’ve avoided sex, and you’ve tasted urine to “check.” If asked about other symptoms, mention occasional morning puffiness, brief crampy belly pain, intermittent tingling, and muscle cramps—say you read online it could be diabetes or calcium. Express misconceptions: “Sweet urine must mean diabetes.” Ask about costs/insurance and fear genetic diagnoses affecting coverage. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, thank them, and share the embarrassing details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sialuria (GNE-related free sialic acid storage disease; autosomal dominant)
acceptable_alternatives: Sialuria (GNE sialic acid feedback inhibition defect)
required_evidence: Markedly increased urine free sialic acid normalized to creatinine with disproportionately elevated free fraction (free >> total pattern consistent with sialuria)
Elevated plasma free sialic acid
Clinical absence of progressive neurodegeneration and lysosomal storage phenotype that would suggest Sandhoff disease
No episodic bradykinin-mediated angioedema pattern (no recurrent swelling without urticaria, no response pattern suggesting hereditary angioedema)
No severe hypertriglyceridemia/chylomicronemia phenotype suggesting familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency
No hypercalcemia/elevated PTH pattern suggesting primary parathyroid hyperplasia
Confirmatory testing plan: molecular testing of GNE (Sialuria-causing variants affecting CMP-sialic acid feedback inhibition) and/or repeat quantitative sialic acid studies

expected_lab_results.Urine free sialic acid (random, normaliz: Markedly elevated (typically several-fold to >10-fold above reference), higher than urine total sialic acid proportionally (high free fraction)
expected_lab_results.Urine total sialic acid (random, normali: Normal to mildly elevated; increase is less pronounced than free sialic acid (free fraction disproportionately high)
expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Usually normal (unless unrelated kidney disease present)

non_pharmacological: Refer to metabolic genetics for confirmatory molecular testing (GNE) and counseling
Provide genetic counseling (autosomal dominant inheritance; discuss family screening and reproductive options)
Baseline clinical assessment for associated features (e.g., mild hepatosplenomegaly, growth parameters, developmental history) and document absence/presence of complications

Avoid unnecessary disease-specific therapies for alternative diagnoses unless supported by evidence (e.g., avoid empiric C1-inhibitor/icatibant without angioedema pattern; avoid lipid-lowering/chylomicronemia protocols without hypertriglyceridemia)
Offer supportive care as needed based on symptoms (e.g., nutrition support if failure to thrive; routine preventive care)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Metabolic genetics appointment within 4-8 weeks; repeat urine/plasma sialic acid quantification and review results within 1-2 months; family screening plan within 3 months

must_include: The abnormal tests suggest increased free sialic acid production/excretion consistent with sialuria
Sialuria is typically inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and is due to a feedback-control problem in sialic acid synthesis (GNE-related)
This condition is often milder and does not match the progressive neurodegenerative course expected in some lysosomal storage diseases
There is no established curative medication; management is supportive and focused on monitoring and genetics follow-up
Next step is confirmatory genetic testing and specialist evaluation
Avoid starting treatments aimed at other conditions unless additional evidence emerges
Discuss what the abnormal urine/plasma results mean and limitations (need confirmation and correlation with clinical findings)

Review red-flag symptoms that should prompt urgent care (e.g., new neurologic regression, recurrent severe swelling/airway symptoms, severe abdominal pain with swelling, pancreatitis symptoms if hypertriglyceridemia suspected)
Invite questions about inheritance, prognosis, and family testing
General healthy diet and routine preventive care; no specific diet proven to treat sialuria
Encourage keeping copies of lab results and sharing with family members and clinicians
If any new medications are prescribed in the future, remind clinicians about penicillin allergy (urticarial rash history)

diagnosis.primary: developmental delay, intellectual disability, obesity, and dysmorphic features

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (genetic syndrome causing developmental delay/intellectual disability, hyperphagia/obesity, and dysmorphic features)
required_evidence: History of developmental delay and/or intellectual disability with characteristic dysmorphic features
Early-onset obesity with hyperphagia/food-seeking behavior (or rapid weight gain out of proportion to intake reported)
Short stature and/or hypogonadism or delayed puberty (if age-appropriate) consistent with hypothalamic dysfunction
Behavioral phenotype (temper outbursts, anxiety/rigidity, skin picking) supporting syndromic obesity
Exclusion of endocrine causes of obesity and confirmation with genetic testing (DNA methylation analysis for 15q11-q13 abnormalities) as the definitive diagnostic step

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: May be normal or elevated; obesity-related insulin resistance may produce prediabetes (5.7–6.4%) or diabetes (>=6.5%)
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: May be normal or elevated (impaired fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL or diabetes >=126 mg/dL) depending on degree of insulin resistance
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated consistent with insulin resistance
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be mildly elevated consistent with metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) risk

medications: Semaglutide 0.25 mg subcutaneous weekly (titrate every 4 weeks as tolerated to 1 mg weekly for diabetes or up to 2.4 mg weekly for obesity per indication/coverage)
Metformin 500 mg PO daily with food (titrate to 1000 mg PO twice daily as tolerated) if prediabetes/diabetes or significant insulin resistance is present
non_pharmacological: Initiate structured, caregiver-supervised food security plan (locked food access, scheduled meals/snacks, consistent rules) and referral to dietitian experienced in syndromic obesity
Physical activity plan tailored to developmental level (goal >=150 minutes/week moderate activity if feasible; include strength activities 2 days/week)
Screen and manage obstructive sleep apnea (sleep study if symptoms or high suspicion) and optimize sleep hygiene
Behavioral/psychiatric support for anxiety/rigidity and food-seeking behaviors (referral to psychology/behavior therapy; consider psychiatry if severe)
Genetic confirmation and counseling (DNA methylation testing; discuss recurrence risk and resources/support groups)

Assess for endocrine comorbidities common in Prader-Willi syndrome (thyroid function, adrenal insufficiency risk if symptomatic, hypogonadism evaluation) and consider endocrinology referral; consider growth hormone therapy evaluation if criteria met
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to review tolerability/adherence and weight trajectory; repeat metabolic labs in 3 months (A1c/glucose, lipids, ALT) and adjust therapy

must_include: This is a syndromic cause of obesity and developmental differences; weight gain is not simply willpower-related
Genetic testing (DNA methylation) is the confirmatory test and guides counseling/support
Common associated issues to screen for (sleep apnea, diabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver disease, hormonal issues, behavioral concerns)
Primary management is structured environment/food security plus activity and behavioral supports; medications can help metabolic complications and appetite/weight
How to take/receive prescribed medications and expected benefits timeline
Key adverse effects and when to seek care (e.g., persistent severe abdominal pain/vomiting for pancreatitis risk with GLP-1 RA; GI side effects; signs of dehydration)
Acknowledge caregiver burden and discuss practical home strategies (locking food, predictable routines, school coordination)
Discuss realistic goals (health/functional goals, gradual weight change) and need for long-term follow-up

Clarify that differentials like central diabetes insipidus are considered if there is polyuria/polydipsia; current available labs assess metabolic risk but DI requires different testing if symptoms emerge
Nutrition: calorie control with high-fiber, protein-forward meals; limit sugar-sweetened beverages; structured portions
Activity: daily movement plan adapted to ability; reduce sedentary time
Sleep and routine: consistent sleep schedule; screen for snoring/daytime sleepiness

ORDER_LAB: A1c/fasting glucose, fasting lipid panel, ALT/AST (fatty liver), CMP, TSH/free T4 (if indicated), consider morning cortisol/IGF-1/sex hormones based on age/puberty, consider vitamin D

correct_diagnosis.primary: Zika virus infectious disease

Exclude/resolve cross-reactivity with other flaviviruses: dengue IgM and dengue PRNT interpretation alongside Zika PRNT

expected_lab_results.Zika virus IgM (serum, MAC-ELISA): Positive (elevated IgM consistent with recent infection; requires confirmatory PRNT due to flavivirus cross-reactivity)
expected_lab_results.Zika virus RNA PCR (serum): May be positive early in illness (typically within ~7 days of symptom onset); otherwise negative
expected_lab_results.Zika virus RNA PCR (urine): May be positive early and can persist longer than serum (often up to ~14 days); otherwise negative
expected_lab_results.Zika PRNT (neutralizing antibody titer): Positive neutralizing antibodies; titer pattern consistent with Zika infection (Zika titer higher than dengue or diagnostic per reference lab criteria)
expected_lab_results.Dengue virus IgM: Negative or indeterminate/low-positive due to cross-reactivity (must be adjudicated with PRNT)
expected_lab_results.Dengue PRNT (neutralizing antibody titer: Negative or lower than Zika PRNT if Zika is the true infection; may be positive if prior flavivirus exposure/vaccination
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal or mildly low (mild leukopenia can occur); no specific diagnostic pattern

non_pharmacological: Supportive care: rest, oral hydration

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours if worsening symptoms; otherwise follow up in 1 week for lab result review/confirmatory testing and counseling (earlier and ongoing follow-up if pregnant).

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 28-year-old male IT support specialist on night shift. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about being judged and about costs/insurance. Start by saying you have “weird sleep episodes” where you suddenly jerk awake, sit up or thrash, sometimes several times a night; your partner says you look awake but don’t respond normally; you feel wiped out next day; it’s been years but getting more frequent.

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE) / Sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (SHE)
required_evidence: Recurrent stereotyped brief nocturnal motor events (hypermotor/dystonic behaviors) arising predominantly from sleep
High event frequency with clustering over nights and abrupt onset/offset
Preserved awareness or partial recall may occur; postictal confusion typically minimal/brief
Semiology suggestive of frontal lobe origin (complex motor behaviors, vocalization, dystonic posturing)
Exclusion of key mimics: parasomnias (NREM arousal disorders), REM sleep behavior disorder, panic attacks, psychogenic nonepileptic events
Supportive testing plan: overnight video-EEG with polysomnography (recognizing interictal EEG may be normal) and/or epilepsy specialist evaluation
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant pattern (if available)

expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (WBC): Typically normal at baseline (no specific diagnostic abnormality expected)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel (Sodium): Typically normal at baseline; establish baseline prior to antiseizure therapy

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100-200 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to typical target 400-800 mg/day in divided doses (or bedtime-focused dosing if events strictly nocturnal)
If carbamazepine not tolerated/contraindicated: Oxcarbazepine 150 mg PO at bedtime; titrate by 150-300 mg every 3-7 days to 600-1200 mg/day
If inadequate response or intolerance to sodium-channel agents: Lacosamide 50 mg PO twice daily; increase weekly to 100-200 mg PO twice daily as needed/tolerated
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with overnight video-EEG with polysomnography and referral to epilepsy specialist
Seizure safety counseling: avoid sleep deprivation, alcohol binges, and recreational drugs; maintain regular sleep schedule
Driving and occupational safety counseling per local regulations for epilepsy/nocturnal seizures; discuss risks with operating heavy machinery, heights, swimming alone
Keep a nocturnal event diary (timing, triggers, semiology, injuries) and consider bedroom safety measures (remove sharp objects, low bed, padding if needed)
Screen for and manage comorbid sleep disorders (e.g., obstructive sleep apnea) that can worsen nocturnal seizures
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Follow up in 2-4 weeks for titration and side-effect check; sooner (within 1 week) if rash, severe dizziness/ataxia, syncope, or worsening seizures; ongoing neurology follow-up every 3 months until stable

must_include: Events are most consistent with nocturnal focal seizures from the frontal lobe (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy), not typical nightmares
These seizures can have normal routine EEGs; overnight video-EEG/PSG helps confirm and exclude parasomnias
Condition can be familial (autosomal dominant) but many patients do well with treatment
First-line therapy often uses sodium-channel antiseizure medications (carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine); goal is to reduce/stop nocturnal events and prevent injury
Titration plan and what to do if a dose is missed (do not double up; take next dose as scheduled unless close to next dose)
Common side effects (dizziness, drowsiness, nausea) and urgent red flags (rash, facial swelling, trouble breathing, severe unsteadiness, fainting)
Discuss differential diagnoses and why they are less likely (NREM parasomnia, REM sleep behavior disorder, panic, psychogenic events)
Discuss safety at night and injury prevention strategies
Discuss reproductive/teratogenic considerations if applicable and need for individualized counseling (e.g., contraception interactions with enzyme-inducing ASMs)
Sleep hygiene and trigger avoidance (sleep deprivation, alcohol, missed doses)
Driving/workplace safety guidance and legal reporting considerations vary by jurisdiction
Encourage adherence, event diary, and when to seek emergency care (prolonged seizure, repeated seizures without recovery, serious injury, new neurologic deficits)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit nocturnal event phenomenology (timing in night, frequency, duration, semiology such as abrupt arousal, hypermotor movements, vocalization), awareness, post-ictal confusion, injuries, incontinence/tongue bite, and triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol).
Clarify differential features vs parasomnias/RBD/panic/PNES (dream enactment, recall, stereotypy, clustering, occurrence in NREM vs REM, daytime events, psychosocial stressors).
Collect key history: age at onset, family history of similar nocturnal events/epilepsy (autosomal dominant pattern), past neurologic history, head trauma, CNS infection, developmental history.
Medication and substance review (current meds incl. antidepressants/antipsychotics, OTC/herbals), alcohol/cannabis/stimulants; pregnancy potential/contraception use if applicable.

Order confirmatory testing appropriate for suspected sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy and to exclude mimics: overnight video-EEG with polysomnography (or prolonged video-EEG); consider brain MRI epilepsy protocol if not previously done.
Review available results and acknowledge that routine EEG may be normal; use overnight video-EEG/PSG for higher yield and parasomnia exclusion.
Make/communicate working diagnosis: nocturnal focal seizures from frontal lobe (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy), with possible autosomal dominant familial pattern, while noting confirmation plan.
Critical safety check before prescribing sodium-channel antiseizure medication (e.g., carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine) to avoid hypersensitivity reactions.
Critical safety check before prescribing due to enzyme induction (carbamazepine) and interactions (e.g., oral contraceptives, warfarin, macrolides, azoles) and additive CNS depression.
Assess contraindications and risk factors relevant to chosen ASM (history of severe cutaneous adverse reactions, hepatic disease, hyponatremia risk, cardiac conduction disease, pregnancy).

Initiate first-line therapy (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine) with a titration plan; include monitoring plan (e.g., sodium for oxcarbazepine, CBC/LFT for carbamazepine per local practice) and clear missed-dose instructions.

Explain diagnosis and differential; counsel on side effects/red flags; safety at night; lifestyle triggers; driving/work restrictions; reproductive counseling and contraception interaction risk with enzyme-inducing ASMs; emergency precautions.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, tolerability, review study results, and adjust therapy; provide earlier follow-up if events persist or adverse effects occur.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated; elevation suggests NAFLD risk
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated; may rise with NAFLD but typically less than ALT

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH, WHO Group 1 PAH)
acceptable_alternatives: Heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension (if family history/genetics later positive)
required_evidence: Symptoms/signs consistent with PAH (e.g., progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest pressure/syncope, edema)

No alternative cause identified for pulmonary hypertension (exclude left heart disease, chronic lung disease/hypoxia, chronic thromboembolic disease, and secondary causes such as connective tissue disease, HIV, portal HTN, drugs/toxins)
Objective evidence of right ventricular strain/dysfunction (e.g., elevated BNP/NT-proBNP, clinical right heart failure findings)

Confirmation of pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension on right-heart catheterization: mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg, pulmonary vascular resistance >2 Wood units

expected_lab_results.BNP: Often elevated; higher values correlate with right ventricular strain and worse prognosis
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Often elevated; used for risk stratification and monitoring response
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Usually normal or mildly elevated; persistent elevation suggests RV ischemia/advanced disease and higher risk
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal; can be elevated if chronic hypoxemia or low if iron deficiency/anemia contributing to dyspnea
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Mirrors hemoglobin; may be elevated with secondary erythrocytosis or low with anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (not a primary feature of IPAH)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Typically normal; thrombocytopenia may occur with advanced right heart failure/congestion or medication effects
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low (hyponatremia) in advanced right heart failure and is a poor prognostic sign

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (titrate to euvolemia for right-sided congestion/edema)
Spironolactone 25 mg PO daily (if volume overload; monitor potassium/renal function)
Ambrisentan 5 mg PO daily (endothelin receptor antagonist; if not pregnant and no significant hepatic disease)
Tadalafil 40 mg PO daily (PDE-5 inhibitor; avoid nitrates/riociguat)
Oxygen via nasal cannula to maintain SpO2 >= 92% (if hypoxemic at rest/exertion/sleep)
non_pharmacological: Refer to a pulmonary hypertension specialty center for risk stratification and therapy escalation planning
Baseline and serial functional assessment (WHO functional class, 6-minute walk distance) and RV monitoring
Vaccinations: annual influenza and age-appropriate pneumococcal vaccination
Supervised low-intensity exercise/pulmonary rehab as tolerated; avoid heavy isometric exertion
Avoid pregnancy; provide contraception counseling (PAH carries high maternal mortality; ERAs are teratogenic)
Avoid high altitude and situations causing hypoxemia when possible
Low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 4-12 weeks after therapy initiation or change, then every 3-6 months (earlier if worsening symptoms).

must_include: PAH is high blood pressure in the lung arteries causing strain on the right side of the heart
Idiopathic means no secondary cause has been found after evaluation
Discuss expected symptoms, disease course, and importance of specialty follow-up and monitoring
Goals: improve symptoms/exercise tolerance, slow progression, reduce hospitalization and mortality risk
Medication classes and purpose (ERA + PDE-5 inhibitor as disease-targeted therapy; diuretics for swelling; oxygen if low O2)
Key adverse effects and monitoring (e.g., ERA teratogenicity and edema; PDE-5 headache/hypotension; diuretic electrolyte issues)
Review allergy history (sulfonamide antibiotics rash; codeine causes nausea/vomiting) and ensure planned meds are compatible
When to seek urgent care: syncope, worsening chest pain, rapidly increasing edema, severe dyspnea, signs of low blood pressure
Set expectations for follow-up testing and possible escalation (e.g., parenteral prostacyclin if high risk)
Activity guidance: paced activity, avoid heavy lifting/straining; consider supervised exercise
Avoid pregnancy and discuss reliable contraception; avoid estrogen-containing methods if high thrombosis risk (individualize)
Diet/fluids: low sodium, daily weights if fluid retention; avoid excess alcohol; avoid smoking
Medication adherence and avoiding interacting drugs (no nitrates with PDE-5; avoid riociguat with PDE-5)

Family history of similar attacks (supports familial cluster headache)
Features less consistent with trigeminal neuralgia: not primarily triggered by touch/chewing, not brief electric-shock pains lasting seconds

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP)
acceptable_alternatives: Complicated hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP) phenotype pending genetic confirmation

required_evidence: Progressive, predominantly lower-limb spasticity and weakness with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantar responses)
Spastic gait with possible urinary urgency and mild sensory complaints but no clear fatigable weakness pattern
Absence of prominent ocular/bulbar fatigability (argues against postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome)
No primary myopathic pattern (no proximal weakness predominance, no marked muscle atrophy early, no myalgia; argues against myofibrillar/distal myopathy)
Family history consistent with inherited disorder OR early onset/slow progression suggestive of genetic spastic paraplegia
Neuroimaging and/or electrophysiology used to exclude structural myelopathy, inflammatory myelitis, B12 deficiency, and motor neuron disease (as part of workup)

expected_lab_results.AST: Typically normal (baseline if considering hepatically metabolized agents)

or Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime, increase by 2 mg every 3-7 days to 2-4 mg up to three times daily as tolerated (max 36 mg/day; monitor LFTs and sedation/hypotension)
Botulinum toxin type A IM to focal spastic muscles per spasticity pattern (e.g., gastrocnemius/soleus, hip adductors) every ~12 weeks if focal spasticity limits gait
Polyethylene glycol 17 g PO daily as needed for constipation related to reduced mobility or antispasticity therapy
non_pharmacological: Physical therapy focused on stretching, strengthening, gait training, and spasticity management; daily home stretching program (hamstrings, hip flexors, calves)
Occupational therapy for ADLs, energy conservation, and adaptive equipment
Assistive devices as indicated (ankle-foot orthoses for foot drop/equinus, cane/walker) and fall-risk assessment with home safety modifications
Bladder symptom screening; timed voiding and referral to urology if urgency/incontinence present
Genetic counseling and genetic testing panel for HSP (e.g., SPAST/SPG4, ATL1, KIF1A, etc.) tailored to phenotype and family history

Consider intrathecal baclofen pump evaluation if severe generalized spasticity refractory to oral therapy or intolerable side effects
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for spasticity, gait function, side effects, and titration; sooner (1-2 weeks) if significant sedation, falls, hypotension, or weakness occurs

must_include: HSP is a group of genetic conditions causing slowly progressive stiffness and weakness of the legs from upper motor neuron pathway involvement
Diagnosis is clinical plus exclusion of mimics; genetic testing can confirm and guide family counseling but may not always find a mutation

Goals: reduce spasticity, improve walking and safety, manage symptoms (pain, cramps, bladder issues), maintain function
Medication plan with titration and common side effects (baclofen/tizanidine: sleepiness, dizziness, weakness; tizanidine: low blood pressure and liver enzyme elevation)

Fall prevention and home safety (remove tripping hazards, appropriate footwear, consider shower bars)
Regular stretching and strengthening routine; avoid prolonged immobility

differential: distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness
required_args.rationale: Progressive bilateral leg spasticity/weakness with UMN pattern; exclude mimics via history, exam features, and screening tests; consider genetic confirmation.

tool_call_sequence.description: Make a clinically supported diagnosis and document consideration of key differentials and why they are less likely.

content_points: HSP is a group of genetic conditions causing slowly progressive stiffness and weakness of the legs from upper motor neuron pathway involvement
Diagnosis is clinical plus exclusion of mimics; genetic testing can confirm and guide family counseling but may not always find a mutation

Differentials considered and why less likely: myopathy (would show primary muscle weakness/atrophy and often elevated CK), congenital myasthenic syndrome (fatigable weakness, ocular/bulbar involvement), distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness (prominent distal weakness and voice/breathing issues)

tool_call_sequence.description: Meet diagnosis-communication milestone with accurate explanation, role/limits of genetic testing, and explicit differential reasoning.

content_points: Discuss prognosis as typically slowly progressive with variable severity; many remain ambulatory for years depending on subtype
Review red flags that should prompt urgent evaluation (new rapid progression, severe back pain, new bowel/bladder retention, new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, marked fatigability)
Shared decision-making on therapy intensity, work/safety, and driving/fall risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Address prognosis, safety red flags, and shared decisions regarding function, work, and fall/driving risk.

tizanidine

tool_call_sequence.description: Critical safety check before prescribing spasticity medications.
Critical drug interaction check before prescribing; particularly CNS depressants, antihypertensives, and CYP1A2 inhibitors (tizanidine).
Collect contraindication-relevant history needed for safe prescribing and counseling (sedation, hypotension, liver disease).

medication_plan_options.name: tizanidine
medication_plan_options.titration: Increase by 2 mg every 3–7 days to 2 mg TID or lowest effective dose

required_args.selection_logic: Choose based on comorbidities, interaction profile, hypotension risk, daytime sedation needs, and patient preference.

nl_assertions: The agent elicits a clear timeline and progression pattern, distinguishing slowly progressive spastic paraparesis from acute/subacute myelopathy.

The agent explicitly screens for red flags (rapid progression, severe back pain, new urinary retention/bowel dysfunction, bulbar/respiratory symptoms, marked fatigability) and states escalation/urgent evaluation when present.
The agent asks about family history and age of onset and explains the genetic nature of HSP without overpromising genetic test yield.

The agent considers and explains why key differentials are less likely, specifically addressing myopathy, congenital myasthenic syndrome, and distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness using discriminating features.
The agent orders or justifies appropriate tests to exclude treatable mimics (at minimum CK and B12; broader labs acceptable) and interprets results coherently.
Before any prescription, the agent performs critical safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure on any is a critical error.

If selecting tizanidine, the agent addresses hypotension risk, CYP1A2 interaction risk, and liver monitoring; if selecting baclofen, the agent warns against abrupt discontinuation and discusses sedation/weakness.
The treatment plan prioritizes non-pharmacologic management (PT/stretching, orthotics, assistive devices, fall prevention) and frames medication as adjunctive.
The agent uses shared decision-making and addresses functional goals (walking safety, work, driving, fall risk) with patient-centered options.
The agent provides a follow-up plan with monitoring parameters and return precautions, including medication side effects and red flags.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 21-year-old anxious but clear-speaking new mom. Start with: “My newborn has had bad diarrhea and a really angry rash since the first couple of weeks.” Volunteer: watery frequent stools many times/day (sometimes mucus), diaper rash that looks red/raw/burning and keeps coming back, baby not gaining weight/looks skinnier, fussier and hard to settle especially after feeds. If asked, add: occasional blood streaks, more spit-up and discomfort during feeds, on-and-off low-grade fevers, thrush that returned after treatment, dry red patches on scalp/behind ears, sometimes very tired and less interested in feeding. Do not mention unless directly asked: fewer wet diapers, sometimes sunken soft spot, cries with few tears, very dry lips/cracked mouth corners, cousin with “immune problems,” your past painful swollen joints/red eyes. Be reluctant but admit if pressed: painful open sores around bottom, bad smell, and you gave leftover antibiotics for a couple days because you panicked. Express misconceptions: think it’s “milk allergy,” switched formulas; think rash is yeast; worry about a parasite from visiting relatives; mention brief staring spells when very tired. If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit fear of bills, missing work, and ask if OTC creams/formulas are basically the same.

diagnosis.primary: neonatal inflammatory skin and bowel disease
differentials: inflammatory bowel disease, immunodeficiency, and encephalopathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Neonatal-onset inflammatory bowel disease due to IL-10/IL-10 receptor pathway deficiency (monogenic very-early-onset IBD) with inflammatory dermatitis
acceptable_alternatives: Neonatal inflammatory skin and bowel disease (NISBD)
required_evidence: Neonatal/early infancy onset of severe inflammatory diarrhea/colitis (often with blood and poor growth) plus inflammatory dermatitis/folliculitis/perianal disease
Marked systemic inflammation (elevated CRP/ESR) with possible anemia and thrombocytosis on CBC
Exclusion of infectious colitis (including amoebiasis) and secondary immunodeficiency as alternate explanations
Clinical severity and early onset consistent with monogenic IBD; need for urgent gastroenterology/immunology evaluation and genetic testing for IL-10/IL-10R pathway

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis) consistent with systemic inflammation; may show neutrophilia
expected_lab_results.CBC Hemoglobin: Low (anemia of inflammation and/or iron deficiency from chronic intestinal blood loss)
expected_lab_results.CBC MCV: Low to normal (microcytic if iron deficiency present; can be normocytic in inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CBC Platelets: Elevated (reactive thrombocytosis in active intestinal inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel Sodium: Normal to low if significant diarrhea/dehydration
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel Potassium: Normal to low if ongoing diarrheal losses

medications: 0.9% Sodium chloride 10-20 mL/kg IV bolus once (repeat as needed for dehydration/shock)
Prednisolone 1 mg/kg PO once daily (short course as bridge under specialist guidance for severe inflammatory colitis)
Zinc sulfate (elemental zinc) 1 mg/kg/day PO daily
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain
non_pharmacological: Urgent admission for neonatal severe colitis evaluation and supportive care (hydration, electrolyte monitoring, strict I/Os, daily weights)
Prompt gastroenterology + immunology consultation; initiate workup for monogenic VEO-IBD (including IL-10/IL-10R genetic testing) and immunodeficiency evaluation
Infectious stool evaluation (bacterial culture/PCR, C. difficile if applicable, ova/parasites with specific testing for Entamoeba histolytica; consider blood cultures if febrile/toxic)
Nutrition support: high-calorie feeding plan; consider elemental formula; assess for failure to thrive
Perianal/skin care: barrier creams, gentle cleansing; consider dermatology input for inflammatory dermatitis/folliculitis
Discuss definitive therapy early: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is potentially curative for IL-10/IL-10R deficiency; coordinate referral to transplant center
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient management; reassess within 24 hours for clinical response and hydration/electrolytes, with specialist follow-up arranged within 1 week after discharge (or sooner if ongoing severe symptoms).

must_include: Condition involves severe inflammation of the intestines starting very early in life and can be linked to an immune signaling/genetic problem (IL-10 pathway)
Why other causes must be ruled out urgently (infection such as amoebiasis, other immunodeficiencies)
Expected course and seriousness: risk of dehydration, poor growth, and complications without specialist care
Immediate priorities: hydration, correcting electrolytes, nutrition support, skin/perianal care
Anti-inflammatory therapy may be used short-term under specialists; definitive treatment may require HSCT if IL-10/IL-10R deficiency confirmed
Need for close monitoring and why hospitalization is recommended for severe neonatal colitis
Medication safety with known allergies: avoid penicillins; avoid NSAIDs because they worsen abdominal pain/diarrhea
Red flags requiring urgent help: decreased urine output, lethargy, persistent fever, bloody stools, worsening rash, signs of dehydration
Uncertainty: diagnosis may evolve after genetic and infectious testing; plan will be updated as results return
Feeding/nutrition plan to support growth (frequent feeds; consider elemental formula if advised)
Skin and diaper-area care to reduce irritation and secondary infection risk
Infection precautions and importance of keeping follow-up appointments with gastroenterology/immunology

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused neonatal history and severity to triage (hydration status, stool frequency/blood, fever, feeding, urine output), plus birth history, exposures, family history/consanguinity, prior infections, and current meds.
Initiate urgent evaluation to rule out infection and assess dehydration/electrolytes; begin workup for very-early-onset IBD/immune dysregulation (including IL-10 pathway considerations).
Interpret results to determine need for hospitalization, empiric therapy, and specialist escalation; reassess differential (infectious colitis vs immune-mediated colitis vs other immunodeficiency).
Synthesize findings into a working diagnosis (neonatal inflammatory skin and bowel disease / VEO-IBD phenotype) while explicitly noting competing urgent causes and uncertainty pending tests.
Required safety check before any prescribing; confirm allergy details (e.g., penicillin reaction type) and document.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing; screen for interactions with any current meds/supplements and planned therapies.

Provide immediate supportive management (e.g., rehydration strategy, barrier creams) and avoid contraindicated agents; any anti-inflammatory/antibiotic therapy should align with neonatal safety and specialist guidance.

Meet all communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment priorities and hospitalization rationale, address concerns (allergies/NSAIDs/red flags/uncertainty), and provide lifestyle/skin/feeding guidance.
Arrange urgent gastroenterology/immunology follow-up and specify monitoring plan; if severe, coordinate admission rather than routine follow-up.
Close after confirming caregiver understanding, return precautions, and next steps.

nl_assertions: Agent prioritizes stabilization and severity assessment in a neonate (hydration, perfusion, urine output, mental status) and recommends hospitalization for severe neonatal colitis or dehydration risk.

Agent explicitly rules out urgent infectious etiologies (including bacterial/viral/parasitic causes such as amoebiasis where relevant) before labeling as immune-mediated disease, and communicates diagnostic uncertainty pending results.

Agent considers very-early-onset IBD/immune dysregulation and mentions IL-10/IL-10 receptor pathway defects as a potential genetic/immune signaling cause linked to neonatal onset colitis with perianal/skin disease.

Agent orders an appropriate initial workup: CBC with differential, CMP/electrolytes, inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR), blood culture if febrile, stool studies (culture, ova/parasites, C. difficile if applicable, viral PCR as locally available), fecal calprotectin (if used), urinalysis if dehydration concern, and considers immunology evaluation (Ig levels, lymphocyte subsets) and genetic testing referral.

Agent avoids unsafe or inappropriate medications in neonates and does not recommend NSAIDs for pain/fever in this context; uses acetaminophen if needed and safe, and emphasizes hydration/nutrition/skin care as first-line.
Before prescribing, agent performs required safety checks: confirms allergies (penicillins) and checks contraindications; also checks interactions when prescribing multiple agents.

Contraindication_check is performed before prescribing and includes neonatal age/weight-based dosing appropriateness, renal/hepatic function considerations, dehydration status, and infection risk before immunosuppression.

If antibiotics are considered for suspected infection, agent avoids penicillins in a penicillin-allergic patient and selects an age-appropriate alternative consistent with neonatal sepsis/enteritis guidance and local protocols (or defers to inpatient/specialist management).
Agent explains expected course and seriousness: risk of dehydration, poor growth/failure to thrive, electrolyte derangements, and complications without specialist care.

Agent explains treatment plan clearly: immediate priorities (hydration, electrolytes, nutrition support, skin/perianal care), possible short-term anti-inflammatory therapy under specialists, and that definitive therapy may require HSCT if IL-10/IL-10R deficiency is confirmed.
Agent provides red-flag return precautions: decreased urine output, lethargy, persistent fever, bloody stools, worsening rash/perianal breakdown, poor feeding, signs of dehydration.

Agent provides caregiver-friendly feeding/nutrition guidance (frequent feeds; consider elemental formula only if advised by specialists), diaper/skin care instructions, infection precautions, and emphasizes adherence to gastroenterology/immunology follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Optimal path: triage severity, initiate infectious + dehydration workup, provide supportive care, communicate VEO-IBD/IL-10 pathway possibility, and arrange hospitalization/specialist escalation with required safety checks before any prescriptions.
must_collect: stool frequency and presence of blood/mucus
duration of symptoms and age at onset (neonatal/first weeks)
signs of dehydration (dry mucosa, lethargy, sunken fontanelle)
sick contacts and travel/exposure risks (including parasitic exposure)
birth history (gestational age, NICU stay, antibiotics)
current medications and prior antibiotic exposures
steps: ASK: focused neonatal GI/skin history and severity assessment
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP/electrolytes, CRP/ESR, stool culture/O&P/viral testing as available, blood culture if febrile, consider immunology labs and genetic testing referral
GET_RESULTS: assess dehydration/electrolyte abnormalities and infection markers
DIAGNOSE: working diagnosis of neonatal inflammatory bowel/skin disease (VEO-IBD phenotype) with differential including infection and immunodeficiency
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds/supplements vs planned therapies

PRESCRIBE: supportive care plan (oral/IV rehydration depending on severity, barrier creams, acetaminophen if needed) and avoid NSAIDs/penicillins; defer immunosuppression/antibiotics to inpatient/specialist if indicated
EDUCATE: cover diagnosis, urgency of ruling out infection, seriousness, treatment priorities, HSCT possibility if IL-10/IL-10R deficiency, red flags, feeding/skin care, infection precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: urgent GI/immunology; recommend admission for severe disease
END: confirm understanding and return precautions

minimal_paths.description: Acceptable minimal path when symptoms are mild, no dehydration, stable vitals, and close follow-up is feasible: outpatient workup initiation, strict return precautions, and expedited specialist referral; still must complete safety checks before any prescriptions.
steps: ASK: assess severity and stability; confirm no red flags
ORDER_LAB: stool studies and baseline labs; arrange urgent immunology/GI evaluation
DIAGNOSE: provisional neonatal inflammatory bowel/skin disease vs infection pending tests
PRESCRIBE: conservative supportive measures only (hydration guidance, barrier creams; avoid NSAIDs)
EDUCATE: milestones including uncertainty and red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: same-week GI/immunology; clear ED return plan

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs DIAGNOSE after initial labs return (GET_RESULTS) rather than before, while maintaining explicit differential and urgency to rule out infection.

Agent includes contraindication screening as part of DIAGNOSE/clinical reasoning but still executes the explicit contraindication_check before PRESCRIBE (e.g., via internal checklist within PRESCRIBE step if the benchmark maps it there).

Agent recommends immediate ED transfer/admission directly after ASK due to severe dehydration/ill appearance, then orders labs as part of inpatient evaluation; still completes allergy/contraindication checks before any medications are prescribed.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mercury poisoning
required_evidence: Elevated mercury level in whole blood and/or urine consistent with exposure
Clinical syndrome compatible with mercury toxicity (e.g., neuropsychiatric symptoms, tremor/paresthesias, gingivostomatitis, GI upset) with or without renal findings
Exposure history supporting mercury source (e.g., occupational/industrial, broken thermometer, artisanal gold mining, fish/seafood overconsumption, skin-lightening creams)
Exclusion of key mimics when indicated (Sandhoff disease unlikely in acute/subacute adult presentation; hereditary angioedema presents with episodic swelling without elevated mercury levels)

expected_lab_results.Whole blood mercury: Elevated above reference range; more reflective of recent exposure (especially organic mercury). Markedly elevated levels support diagnosis.
expected_lab_results.Urine mercury (24-hour): Elevated above reference range; best reflects inorganic/elemental exposure and body burden. Higher levels correlate with ongoing exposure and/or significant body burden.
expected_lab_results.Urine mercury (spot, creatinine-correcte: Elevated mercury/creatinine ratio above reference range; useful for screening/monitoring when 24-hour collection is impractical.
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - protein: May show proteinuria (tubular injury) in moderate-to-severe toxicity; can be normal in mild cases.
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - blood: May be negative or show microscopic hematuria in renal involvement; not required for diagnosis.
expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Normal in mild cases; may be elevated if mercury-associated nephrotoxicity is present.
expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal in mild cases; may be elevated with renal impairment/dehydration.
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): Usually normal unless nephrotoxicity; may be reduced if renal involvement.

medications: Dimercaprol (BAL) 3-5 mg/kg IM every 4 hours for 2 days, then every 6 hours for 1 day, then every 12 hours for 7-10 days (consider for severe inorganic/elemental mercury toxicity with significant symptoms; specialist-guided)

Succimer (DMSA) 10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours for 5 days, then 10 mg/kg PO every 12 hours for 14 days (typical outpatient chelation option for symptomatic patients with elevated mercury levels; specialist-guided)
Dimercaptopropane sulfonate (DMPS) 250 mg PO every 8-12 hours (where available; specialist-guided alternative chelator)
non_pharmacological: Immediate removal from exposure source and environmental decontamination (workplace controls, avoid contaminated products, stop high-mercury fish/seafood if relevant)
Contact regional Poison Control / medical toxicology for case-specific chelation indications, agent choice, and monitoring plan
Supportive care based on symptoms (hydration, antiemetics if needed, management of neuropsychiatric symptoms as clinically indicated)
Assess exposure route: if inhalational exposure suspected, evaluate respiratory status; if dermal/cosmetic source, discontinue and ensure safe disposal
Monitor renal function and urinalysis during and after chelation; monitor mercury levels to document decline
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck symptoms, renal function (creatinine/BUN/eGFR), urinalysis, and mercury levels within 1-2 weeks after initiating exposure cessation/chelation, then every 2-4 weeks until clinical improvement and levels trend down; sooner if worsening symptoms.

must_include: Mercury exposure can cause neurologic, GI, oral, and kidney symptoms; lab confirmation uses blood and urine mercury
Different mercury forms behave differently (blood often reflects recent/organic exposure; urine reflects inorganic/elemental exposure)
Why Sandhoff disease and hereditary angioedema are less likely given mercury elevations and clinical pattern
First step is stopping exposure; chelation is reserved for symptomatic and/or significantly elevated levels and should be guided by toxicology
Chelation risks and monitoring needs (renal function/urinalysis, symptom monitoring, repeat mercury levels)
When urgent care is needed (worsening neurologic symptoms, breathing issues, reduced urine output, severe abdominal pain)
Discuss likely exposure sources and practical steps to eliminate them
Set expectations: symptom improvement may take weeks; levels should trend down after exposure cessation and/or chelation
Review penicillin allergy relevance (not directly related to chelators, but must be documented for future prescribing)
Avoid high-mercury fish (e.g., shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish) and follow local advisories if dietary source suspected
Workplace/home safety measures to prevent re-exposure (PPE, ventilation, safe cleanup; avoid vacuuming elemental mercury spills)
Importance of follow-up testing and keeping all mercury-containing products out of reach of children

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit exposure history (fish/occupational/home spills/skin creams), symptom timeline, and red flags; establish baseline meds, pregnancy status, and comorbidities.
Required before any prescribing; document penicillin allergy details and any reactions relevant to future medication choices.
Confirm and characterize mercury burden and assess organ involvement (blood/urine mercury, renal function, urinalysis; consider CBC/CMP).
Interpret mercury levels in context of exposure type and symptoms; determine severity and need for urgent management/toxicology input.
Synthesize exposure + symptoms + labs to diagnose mercury poisoning and document differential reasoning (including why Sandhoff disease and hereditary angioedema are less likely).
Explain diagnosis and treatment principles; emphasize exposure cessation, monitoring, and when chelation is indicated; provide safety/return precautions.
If prescribing chelation or supportive meds, review interactions; non-critical but expected best practice.
Re-confirm allergy status immediately before prescribing to satisfy safety check timing.
Critical before prescribing: ensure no contraindications to chelators/supportive meds (e.g., severe renal impairment for some agents, pregnancy considerations, G6PD if relevant per agent choice).
Only if indicated (symptomatic and/or significantly elevated levels) and ideally in coordination with toxicology; otherwise avoid unnecessary chelation and provide supportive care plan.
Arrange repeat mercury levels and renal monitoring; ensure exposure remediation and symptom reassessment within an appropriate timeframe.
Close after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Mild symptoms and/or modest elevation consistent with recent dietary exposure; manage with exposure cessation, monitoring, and education without chelation.
steps: ASK: exposure history, symptoms, red flags, meds, pregnancy, allergies
ORDER_LAB: blood mercury, urine mercury (spot or 24h as protocol), creatinine/BUN, urinalysis (+/- CBC/CMP)
GET_RESULTS: interpret blood vs urine pattern and severity
DIAGNOSE: mercury poisoning; document why Sandhoff disease and hereditary angioedema are less likely
EDUCATE: stop exposure; dietary and home/work safety; explain form-dependent labs; set expectations; urgent return precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: repeat mercury levels and renal tests; reassess symptoms

minimal_paths.description: Symptomatic patient with clearly elevated mercury levels (or concerning exposure such as elemental inhalation) where chelation is considered; proceed with toxicology-guided plan and monitoring.
steps: ASK: exposure history (including elemental vapor), symptoms, red flags, meds, pregnancy, allergies, renal history
ORDER_LAB: blood and urine mercury, CMP/creatinine, urinalysis (+/- CBC); consider EKG if symptomatic palpitations or severe illness
GET_RESULTS: confirm significant elevation and/or organ involvement
DIAGNOSE: mercury poisoning; document why Sandhoff disease and hereditary angioedema are less likely

contraindication_check: renal impairment/pregnancy/other relevant contraindications for chosen agent
PRESCRIBE: chelator/supportive therapy per toxicology guidance; include monitoring plan
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: repeat mercury levels, renal function/urinalysis, symptom check

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders additional supportive diagnostics when indicated by history (e.g., neuro exam documentation, audiology/vision referral, stool studies only if alternative GI diagnosis suspected) while still prioritizing exposure cessation and mercury confirmation.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 36-year-old anxious but clear-speaking marketing project manager. Open with: “I got this sudden, explosive thunderclap headache—the worst of my life.” Volunteer that it hits out of nowhere, peaks in seconds, has happened several times over the last week (often after exertion or getting upset), with nausea, light sensitivity, and neck stiffness/soreness afterward.

If asked, add triggers (sex, straining, intense exercise), that you feel mostly okay between attacks but dread the next one, and that once you had brief right-hand tingling/clumsiness, brief blurry vision, and paramedics noted high BP. Deny fever/cold symptoms and head injury.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: postpartum 3 weeks with poor sleep; new antidepressant with recent dose increase; decongestant with stimulant; heavy caffeine pre-workout; brief word-finding trouble.

Resist admitting cocaine and increased marijuana; disclose only if asked directly, with embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s a really bad migraine,” worry about meningitis from neck pain, fear aneurysm, wonder if panic/stress is causing it.

Ask about costs, missing work, cheapest meds, and whether follow-up imaging can be skipped if it settles. If the clinician is empathetic, you calm slightly, thank them, and become more willing to answer honestly.

medications: Sertraline 100 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
Sumatriptan 50 mg PO PRN migraine (max 200 mg/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain
confounders: Postpartum period (risk overlap with cerebral venous sinus thrombosis and preeclampsia-related headache)
History of migraine and use of triptan (can mimic/trigger thunderclap headaches and vasoconstriction)
SSRI therapy (serotonergic agent associated with RCVS triggers)
Recent cannabis use (vasoactive trigger for RCVS)
Transient severe hypertension during pain (may suggest hypertensive emergency or PRES)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
acceptable_alternatives: Probable reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (pending vascular imaging confirmation)
required_evidence: Recurrent thunderclap headache(s) peaking within 1 minute, often over days to weeks
No evidence of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage on initial evaluation (e.g., noncontrast head CT ± LP if indicated)

Cerebral arterial vasoconstriction on CTA/MRA/DSA (multifocal segmental narrowing/"string-of-beads") OR high clinical suspicion with plan for repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks if initial imaging is normal
Clinical course consistent with reversibility (symptoms/angiographic changes improve over weeks; follow-up imaging planned within 6–12 weeks)
Exclusion of key mimics: primary angiitis of the CNS, cervical artery dissection, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, pituitary apoplexy, hypertensive emergency/ICH, meningitis/encephalitis

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (no marked leukocytosis expected unless alternative diagnosis/infection)

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours (or 30 mg PO every 2 hours if hypotension) for 2-4 weeks, then taper as symptoms improve
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/PR every 6-8 hours as needed (max 3 g/day if no liver disease; avoid medication overuse)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV every 8 hours as needed for nausea
non_pharmacological: Admit for neurologic monitoring if severe/recurrent thunderclap headaches or any focal deficits; frequent neuro checks
Immediate vascular imaging (CTA or MRA head/neck) and brain imaging; repeat vascular imaging in 1-2 weeks if initial study is negative but suspicion remains

Avoid triggers/vasoactive substances: triptans, ergot derivatives, decongestants (pseudoephedrine), stimulants (cocaine/amphetamines), cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs if suspected precipitant, nicotine, excessive caffeine; avoid abrupt exertion/sexual activity until stabilized
Avoid glucocorticoids (associated with worse outcomes in RCVS unless another diagnosis requires them)
Blood pressure management: avoid severe hypertension; avoid hypotension (supportive, individualized targets)
Screen for postpartum state and medication/substance triggers; discontinue offending agents
Educate on red flags requiring emergent care: new neurologic deficits, persistent/worsening thunderclap headache, seizure, vision loss, confusion
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1 week after discharge (or sooner if symptoms persist), with repeat vascular imaging to document reversibility at 6-12 weeks

must_include: RCVS is a condition where brain arteries temporarily constrict and then relax over weeks
Typical symptom is recurrent thunderclap headache; some patients can develop stroke or bleeding
Diagnosis often relies on vascular imaging and sometimes repeat imaging because early scans can be normal
Urgency: seek emergency care for weakness, speech trouble, vision changes, seizure, or worst/worsening headache
Main treatment is supportive plus a calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) to reduce headache and vasoconstriction
Avoid steroids unless another diagnosis is confirmed
Plan for monitoring and follow-up imaging to confirm reversibility
Discuss potential side effects of nimodipine: low blood pressure, dizziness; advise to report fainting
Discuss expected course: symptoms usually improve over days to weeks; full reversibility expected in most cases
Acknowledge risk of complications (ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage) and why monitoring/imaging is needed
Shared decision-making about admission vs close outpatient monitoring depending on severity and neurologic findings
Stop and avoid vasoactive/trigger substances and medications (triptans, stimulants, decongestants, cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines)
Avoid heavy exertion/sexual activity until cleared; gradual return to activity after stabilization
Avoid medication-overuse headache: limit PRN analgesics and follow dosing guidance
Hydration, sleep, and stress reduction strategies

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected RCVS: thunderclap headache pattern (recurrent), onset/peak, triggers (exertion/sex/Valsalva), postpartum state, vasoactive substances/meds (triptans, SSRIs/SNRIs, stimulants, decongestants, cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines), prior migraine, trauma, infection, and red flags (focal deficits, seizure, altered mental status).

Order baseline evaluation to support differential and medication safety: pregnancy test if relevant, CBC/CMP, coagulation studies if hemorrhage concern, inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) to help distinguish from vasculitis, and toxicology screen if substance use suspected.
Review results for alternative diagnoses and for contraindications/precautions to calcium-channel blocker therapy (e.g., hypotension, hepatic impairment) and to guide disposition.

Order/ensure vascular imaging is obtained (modeled here as an order): CTA/MRA head/neck +/- CT head for hemorrhage; note that early vascular imaging can be normal and may require repeat imaging to demonstrate reversibility.
Confirm imaging findings compatible with RCVS (segmental vasoconstriction) or acknowledge normal early imaging with continued suspicion; assess for complications (ischemic stroke, SAH/ICH).

Make working diagnosis of RCVS (or high suspicion) after excluding emergent mimics and assessing imaging/labs; document differential (aneurysmal SAH, cervical artery dissection, CVST, PACNS/vasculitis, pituitary apoplexy).
Critical safety check before prescribing nimodipine or alternative calcium-channel blocker; verify drug allergies and reaction types.
Non-critical but expected: assess interactions (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers with nimodipine, additive hypotension with other antihypertensives).

Critical contraindication assessment before prescribing: current blood pressure, syncope/orthostasis, severe aortic stenosis/cardiogenic shock, significant hepatic impairment, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, and ability to monitor.

Initiate supportive therapy and a calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) for headache/vasoconstriction when safe; avoid steroids unless another diagnosis is confirmed; provide analgesic plan avoiding overuse.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (including nimodipine side effects and avoiding steroids), address concerns (course/complications/admission vs outpatient), and lifestyle/trigger avoidance counseling.
Arrange neurology follow-up and repeat vascular imaging in coming weeks to confirm reversibility; set monitoring plan and clear return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment plan, follow-up, and emergency return instructions are provided.

Hydrocortisone instructions include split dosing, not stopping abruptly, and stress/sick-day dosing guidance; provides an emergency plan and discusses injectable steroid access/education when applicable.
Levothyroxine instructions include daily consistent timing, separation from binding agents (e.g., iron/calcium), and dose adjustments guided by free T4 rather than TSH.
The agent reviews emergency symptoms requiring urgent care: severe weakness, vomiting, fainting, confusion, severe abdominal pain, and hypotension.
The agent clarifies current testosterone use (indication, benefits/risks, and the need for coordinated endocrine management rather than unsupervised use).

The agent advises medical alert identification for adrenal insufficiency, carrying an updated medication list and emergency steroid instructions, and emphasizes adherence and follow-up labs/visits for titration and safety monitoring.
The agent avoids unsafe reassurance and provides clear safety-net instructions for possible adrenal crisis.
The agent’s plan includes ongoing monitoring and acknowledges possible additional hormone replacements after full pituitary evaluation.

minimal_paths.description: Typical L1 pathway: identify postpartum pituitary failure symptoms, confirm central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism on labs, start hydrocortisone first then levothyroxine, provide education and follow-up.
must_collect: history of severe postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension or complicated delivery
amenorrhea/infertility or loss of menses postpartum
weight change and cold intolerance/constipation (hypothyroid symptoms)
current medications and supplements (especially thyroid meds, steroids, iron/calcium, anticoagulants, diabetes meds)
red flags for adrenal crisis (vomiting, syncope, confusion, severe abdominal pain, hypotension)
steps: ASK focused postpartum and endocrine symptom history + medication review + red flags
ORDER_LAB: 8am cortisol/ACTH, free T4/TSH, CMP (Na/glucose), prolactin, LH/FSH with sex steroids, IGF-1 (and pregnancy test if applicable)
GET_RESULTS and interpret as central adrenal insufficiency + central hypothyroidism consistent with Sheehan syndrome
PRESCRIBE hydrocortisone replacement first with stress-dose plan
PRESCRIBE levothyroxine after steroids started; monitoring by free T4
EDUCATE diagnosis, treatment order, sick-day rules, emergency symptoms, medical alert, testosterone counseling
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP for lab review and dose titration

minimal_paths.description: If red flags suggest impending adrenal crisis, prioritize urgent steroid coverage and emergency referral while still meeting core counseling requirements.
must_collect: severe weakness or inability to keep fluids down
current meds including any thyroid hormone use
steps: ASK to identify adrenal crisis symptoms and immediate risk
ORDER_LAB: CMP (Na/glucose) and cortisol/ACTH if feasible without delaying care
DIAGNOSE suspected Sheehan-related secondary adrenal insufficiency with high concern for adrenal crisis
PRESCRIBE immediate glucocorticoid coverage per protocol (and instruct urgent ED/EMS evaluation for IV steroids/fluids)
EDUCATE emergency symptoms, sick-day rules, and explicitly defer levothyroxine until after steroids are established
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP after stabilization for full pituitary workup and thyroid initiation

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders additional confirmatory/etiology tests and imaging without delaying steroid initiation when adrenal insufficiency is present (e.g., pituitary MRI; other pituitary axes).
steps: ORDER_LAB (includes additional pituitary axes; consider pituitary MRI order)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent starts empiric hydrocortisone after drawing labs when cortisol is low/clinical suspicion is high, and delays levothyroxine until steroid therapy is established; monitoring plan emphasizes free T4 targets.
steps: PRESCRIBE hydrocortisone (after labs drawn if possible)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs allergy and interaction checks before or after diagnosis, but always before prescribing; contraindication check is explicitly completed before prescribing.
